g.data-narration: Welcome to Process Fundamentals. We'll explore how programs become processes and how operating systems manage them.
g.text: Understanding How Programs Execute in Memory

g.data-narration: A process is a program in execution. It's an active entity with program code, current activity, stack, data, and heap.
g.text: A process is a program in execution. It includes:

g.data-narration: A program is passive - stored on disk with no state. A process is active - loaded in memory with running state.
The memory layout shows how process data is organized: text at bottom, then data segments, heap grows up, stack grows down.
The stack stores local variables and manages function calls, growing downward. The heap stores dynamically allocated memory, growing upward.
The Process Control Block (PCB) is a data structure that stores all information about a process. It's the OS's way to track processes.
g.text: The PCB is a kernel data structure containing ALL information about a process:
PCB = { PID, State, PC, Registers, Memory Info, I/O Status, Scheduling Info }

g.data-narration: PCB fields include: PID for unique identification, process state, program counter, CPU registers, memory info, I/O status, and scheduling data.

g.text: Address of next instruction to execute
Current register values (PC, SP, Acc, etc.)

g.data-narration: Parent processes create child processes using system calls, forming a process tree. Resource sharing options vary: all, partial, or none.
The fork() system call creates a new process. The child gets a copy of the parent's address space. Both parent and child continue execution.
The exec() system call replaces the current process's memory with a new program. The PID remains the same but code, data, and stack change.

g.text: PID stays the same, but entire memory is replaced!

g.data-narration: Process termination can be voluntary (normal exit, error exit) or involuntary (fatal error, killed by another process).
Parent processes may wait for children using wait(). Orphan processes have no parent. Zombie processes have terminated but parent hasn't called wait.

g.text: Parent waits for child to finish
Child dead, parent hasn't called wait()

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: Process is active vs program passive, memory has text/data/heap/stack layout, PCB tracks process state, fork creates child, exec replaces program.

g.text: • Process = program in execution (active) vs Program = stored on disk (passive)
• Memory Layout: Text → Data → BSS → Heap(grows up) → Stack(grows down)
• PCB stores all process info: PID, State, PC, Registers, Memory, I/O, Scheduling
• fork() creates child (copy), exec() replaces program
• Parent may wait() for child; orphan vs zombie processes

g.data-narration: Exam tips: Know PCB fields and memory layout diagram. Remember fork() returns 0 to child, child's PID to parent. Distinguish zombie vs orphan processes.

g.text: Memorize the Process Memory Layout diagram (Text at bottom, Stack at top)
Remember: fork() returns 0 to child, returns child's PID to parent
Zombie = dead child waiting for parent to call wait(); Orphan = parent died first
